As a deity of {{ill|lt=brewing|Q869095|WD=Q869095|lt=sake brewing|1=Brewing}}, it is worshiped by {{ill|lt=sake brewers|Q11639726|WD=Q11639726|1=tsukurizakaya}}. === Enshrined deities === ; Main deities * Kusu-no-Kami * {{ill|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Kami|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|1=Oyamakui no Kami}} ; Associated deities * {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Amatsuhikohikohononinigi-no-Mikoto|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}} * Momoyasogami (one hundred eighty deities) Kusu-no-Kami is a deity of {{ill|lt=nihonshu|Q170219|WD=Q170219|lt=sake|1=Sake}}, and is also treated as another name of {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Sukunabikona}}. Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto was invited from {{ill|lt=Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} in Kyoto. Konohanasakuya-hime, an associated deity, is also connected with brewing. In the section on Saka-go in Tate-nui District of the ''{{ill|lt=Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki|Q11395858|WD=Q11395858|1=Izumo no Kuni Fudoki}}'', there is a tale that 180 deities gathered on a river islet, built a sacred kitchen, brewed sake, held a banquet for 180 days, and then dispersed. The associated deity “Momoyasogami” reflects this story. === History === It is identified with “Saka-sha,” a Shrine Bureau shrine in the Tate-nui District section of ''Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki,'' and with “Saka Shrine” in Tate-nui District, Izumo Province, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. In the Kozakai village entry of ''Unyōshi'' (1717, Kyōhō 2), it appears as “Matsuo Myōjin.” That source says there is a ridge tag from {{ill|lt=1500|Q6657|WD=Q6657|1=La Vajol}} ({{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} 9), suggesting the Matsuo Taisha deity was invited in the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} it continued to receive reverence from the lords of the {{ill|lt=Matsue Domain|Q1017072|WD=Q1017072|1=Matsue Domain}} and local {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izumo tōji|1=UNKNOWN}} brewers. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} it restored the name “Saka Shrine,” but the common name “Matsuo Shrine” has remained in parallel use. === Festivals === The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=annual grand festival|1=UNKNOWN}} is held on October 13. It is also called the Doburoku Festival and is said to continue from the Muromachi period. Before dawn on October 1, the chief priest acts as {{ill|lt=tōji|Q11523596|WD=Q11523596|1=Tōji}} and brews sacred sake; on the day before the festival, officials of the {{ill|lt=National Tax Agency|Q11421205|WD=Q11421205|1=National Tax Agency}} inspect it. On the day, sake brewers gather and hold a dedication rite praying for safety. The shrine receives yearly permission from Japan’s Ministry of Finance to brew up to one koku of {{ill|lt=doburoku|Q1371541|WD=Q1371541|1=Doburoku}} for the festival. === References === * Seki Kazuhiko, ''Commentary on Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki'', 2006, {{ill|lt=Akashi Shoten|Q11512528|WD=Q11512528|1=Akashi Shoten}} ISBN 4-7503-2376-4 * Takine Yoshiyuki, ''Dictionary of Ancient Izumo'', 2001, {{ill|lt=Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha|Q11501312|WD=Q11501312|1=Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha. "Rekishi Dokuhon" Henshūbu}} ISBN 4-404-02941-1 * Edited by {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Kenichi|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}}, ''Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places 7 San’in'' (new edition), 2000, {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}} ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 === Related literature === *{{Cite journal |和書|author =Hayami Yasutaka|title =Izumo Myth and Sake Brewing|date =1987|publisher =Brewing Society of Japan|journal =Journal of the Brewing Society of Japan|volume =82|issue =8|doi=10.6013/jbrewsocjapan1915.82.562|pages =562-566|ref = }} === Related items === *{{ill|lt=Matsuo Shrine|Q11529677|WD=Q11529677|1=Matsuo shrine}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Saka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Saka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumo Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:島根県の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumo Province]] [[Category:島根県の旧郷社]] [[Category:出雲市の建築物]] [[Category:History of Izumo, Shimane]] [[Category:島根県の酒]][[Category:qq]]

{'text': '島根県出雲市小境町108', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:佐香神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11385133}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Saka Shrine |画像=[[File:佐香神社（出雲市小境町）.jpg|270px|Saka Shrine]]
Honden (left) and haiden (right) |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Izumo|Q825166|WD=Q825166|1=Izumo, Shimane}} Kozakai-chō 108 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|28|12|N|132|52|50|E|}} |祭神 = Kusu-no-Kami
{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|1=Oyamakui no Kami}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}
former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|lt=taisha-zukuri|Q387874|WD=Q387874|1=Taisha-zukuri}} |別名 = Matsuo Shrine |例祭 = October 13 |神事 = Doburoku Festival |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Saka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Saka Shrine''' (さかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Kozakai-chō, {{ill|lt=Izumo|Q825166|WD=Q825166|1=Izumo, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It is also called '''Matsuo Shrine'''. As a deity of {{ill|lt=brewing|Q869095|WD=Q869095|lt=sake brewing|1=Brewing}}, it is worshiped by {{ill|lt=sake brewers|Q11639726|WD=Q11639726|1=tsukurizakaya}}. === Enshrined deities === ; Main deities * Kusu-no-Kami * {{ill|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Kami|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|1=Oyamakui no Kami}} ; Associated deities * {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Amatsuhikohikohononinigi-no-Mikoto|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}} * Momoyasogami (one hundred eighty deities) Kusu-no-Kami is a deity of {{ill|lt=nihonshu|Q170219|WD=Q170219|lt=sake|1=Sake}}, and is also treated as another name of {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Sukunabikona}}. Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto was invited from {{ill|lt=Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} in Kyoto. Konohanasakuya-hime, an associated deity, is also connected with brewing. In the section on Saka-go in Tate-nui District of the ''{{ill|lt=Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki|Q11395858|WD=Q11395858|1=Izumo no Kuni Fudoki}}'', there is a tale that 180 deities gathered on a river islet, built a sacred kitchen, brewed sake, held a banquet for 180 days, and then dispersed. The associated deity “Momoyasogami” reflects this story. === History === It is identified with “Saka-sha,” a Shrine Bureau shrine in the Tate-nui District section of ''Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki,'' and with “Saka Shrine” in Tate-nui District, Izumo Province, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. In the Kozakai village entry of ''Unyōshi'' (1717, Kyōhō 2), it appears as “Matsuo Myōjin.” That source says there is a ridge tag from {{ill|lt=1500|Q6657|WD=Q6657|1=La Vajol}} ({{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} 9), suggesting the Matsuo Taisha deity was invited in the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} it continued to receive reverence from the lords of the {{ill|lt=Matsue Domain|Q1017072|WD=Q1017072|1=Matsue Domain}} and local {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izumo tōji|1=UNKNOWN}} brewers. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} it restored the name “Saka Shrine,” but the common name “Matsuo Shrine” has remained in parallel use. === Festivals === The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=annual grand festival|1=UNKNOWN}} is held on October 13. It is also called the Doburoku Festival and is said to continue from the Muromachi period. Before dawn on October 1, the chief priest acts as {{ill|lt=tōji|Q11523596|WD=Q11523596|1=Tōji}} and brews sacred sake; on the day before the festival, officials of the {{ill|lt=National Tax Agency|Q11421205|WD=Q11421205|1=National Tax Agency}} inspect it. On the day, sake brewers gather and hold a dedication rite praying for safety. The shrine receives yearly permission from Japan’s Ministry of Finance to brew up to one koku of {{ill|lt=doburoku|Q1371541|WD=Q1371541|1=Doburoku}} for the festival. === References === * Seki Kazuhiko, ''Commentary on Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki'', 2006, {{ill|lt=Akashi Shoten|Q11512528|WD=Q11512528|1=Akashi Shoten}} ISBN 4-7503-2376-4 * Takine Yoshiyuki, ''Dictionary of Ancient Izumo'', 2001, {{ill|lt=Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha|Q11501312|WD=Q11501312|1=Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha. "Rekishi Dokuhon" Henshūbu}} ISBN 4-404-02941-1 * Edited by {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Kenichi|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}}, ''Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places 7 San’in'' (new edition), 2000, {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}} ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 === Related literature === *{{Cite journal |和書|author =Hayami Yasutaka|title =Izumo Myth and Sake Brewing|date =1987|publisher =Brewing Society of Japan|journal =Journal of the Brewing Society of Japan|volume =82|issue =8|doi=10.6013/jbrewsocjapan1915.82.562|pages =562-566|ref = }} === Related items === *{{ill|lt=Matsuo Shrine|Q11529677|WD=Q11529677|1=Matsuo shrine}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Saka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Saka Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumo Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumo Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:出雲市の建築物]] [[Category:History of Izumo, Shimane]] [[Category:島根県の酒]][[Category:qq]]

Honden (left) and haiden (right) |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Izumo|Q825166|WD=Q825166|1=Izumo, Shimane}} Kozakai-chō 108 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|28|12|N|132|52|50|E|}} |祭神 = Kusu-no-Kami
{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|1=Oyamakui no Kami}} |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}
former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = |本殿 = {{ill|lt=taisha-zukuri|Q387874|WD=Q387874|1=Taisha-zukuri}} |別名 = Matsuo Shrine |例祭 = October 13 |神事 = Doburoku Festival |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shimane|label=Saka Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Saka Shrine''' (さかじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Kozakai-chō, {{ill|lt=Izumo|Q825166|WD=Q825166|1=Izumo, Shimane}}, {{ill|lt=Shimane Prefecture|Q132751|WD=Q132751|1=Shimane Prefecture}}. It is also called '''Matsuo Shrine'''. As a deity of {{ill|lt=brewing|Q869095|WD=Q869095|lt=sake brewing|1=Brewing}}, it is worshiped by {{ill|lt=sake brewers|Q11639726|WD=Q11639726|1=tsukurizakaya}}. === Enshrined deities === ; Main deities * Kusu-no-Kami * {{ill|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Kami|Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto|1=Oyamakui no Kami}} ; Associated deities * {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Amatsuhikohikohononinigi-no-Mikoto|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}} * Momoyasogami (one hundred eighty deities) Kusu-no-Kami is a deity of {{ill|lt=nihonshu|Q170219|WD=Q170219|lt=sake|1=Sake}}, and is also treated as another name of {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Sukunabikona}}. Ōyamakui-no-Mikoto was invited from {{ill|lt=Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} in Kyoto. Konohanasakuya-hime, an associated deity, is also connected with brewing. In the section on Saka-go in Tate-nui District of the ''{{ill|lt=Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki|Q11395858|WD=Q11395858|1=Izumo no Kuni Fudoki}}'', there is a tale that 180 deities gathered on a river islet, built a sacred kitchen, brewed sake, held a banquet for 180 days, and then dispersed. The associated deity “Momoyasogami” reflects this story. === History === It is identified with “Saka-sha,” a Shrine Bureau shrine in the Tate-nui District section of ''Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki,'' and with “Saka Shrine” in Tate-nui District, Izumo Province, in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}. In the Kozakai village entry of ''Unyōshi'' (1717, Kyōhō 2), it appears as “Matsuo Myōjin.” That source says there is a ridge tag from {{ill|lt=1500|Q6657|WD=Q6657|1=La Vajol}} ({{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} 9), suggesting the Matsuo Taisha deity was invited in the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} it continued to receive reverence from the lords of the {{ill|lt=Matsue Domain|Q1017072|WD=Q1017072|1=Matsue Domain}} and local {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izumo tōji|1=UNKNOWN}} brewers. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} it restored the name “Saka Shrine,” but the common name “Matsuo Shrine” has remained in parallel use. === Festivals === The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=annual grand festival|1=UNKNOWN}} is held on October 13. It is also called the Doburoku Festival and is said to continue from the Muromachi period. Before dawn on October 1, the chief priest acts as {{ill|lt=tōji|Q11523596|WD=Q11523596|1=Tōji}} and brews sacred sake; on the day before the festival, officials of the {{ill|lt=National Tax Agency|Q11421205|WD=Q11421205|1=National Tax Agency}} inspect it. On the day, sake brewers gather and hold a dedication rite praying for safety. The shrine receives yearly permission from Japan’s Ministry of Finance to brew up to one koku of {{ill|lt=doburoku|Q1371541|WD=Q1371541|1=Doburoku}} for the festival. === References === * Seki Kazuhiko, ''Commentary on Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki'', 2006, {{ill|lt=Akashi Shoten|Q11512528|WD=Q11512528|1=Akashi Shoten}} ISBN 4-7503-2376-4 * Takine Yoshiyuki, ''Dictionary of Ancient Izumo'', 2001, {{ill|lt=Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha|Q11501312|WD=Q11501312|1=Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha. "Rekishi Dokuhon" Henshūbu}} ISBN 4-404-02941-1 * Edited by {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Kenichi|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}}, ''Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places 7 San’in'' (new edition), 2000, {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}} ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 === Related literature === *{{Cite journal |和書|author =Hayami Yasutaka|title =Izumo Myth and Sake Brewing|date =1987|publisher =Brewing Society of Japan|journal =Journal of the Brewing Society of Japan|volume =82|issue =8|doi=10.6013/jbrewsocjapan1915.82.562|pages =562-566|ref = }} === Related items === *{{ill|lt=Matsuo Shrine|Q11529677|WD=Q11529677|1=Matsuo shrine}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Saka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Saka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumo Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumo Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:出雲市の建築物]] [[Category:History of Izumo, Shimane]] [[Category:島根県の酒]]

In the Kozakai village entry of ''Unyōshi'' (1717, Kyōhō 2), it appears as “Matsuo Myōjin.” That source says there is a ridge tag from {{ill|lt=1500|Q6657|WD=Q6657|1=La Vajol}} ({{ill|lt=Meiō|Q1193841|WD=Q1193841|1=Meiō}} 9), suggesting the Matsuo Taisha deity was invited in the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|WD=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}. In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} it continued to receive reverence from the lords of the {{ill|lt=Matsue Domain|Q1017072|WD=Q1017072|1=Matsue Domain}} and local {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Izumo tōji|1=UNKNOWN}} brewers. In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} it restored the name “Saka Shrine,” but the common name “Matsuo Shrine” has remained in parallel use. === Festivals === The {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=annual grand festival|1=UNKNOWN}} is held on October 13. It is also called the Doburoku Festival and is said to continue from the Muromachi period. Before dawn on October 1, the chief priest acts as {{ill|lt=tōji|Q11523596|WD=Q11523596|1=Tōji}} and brews sacred sake; on the day before the festival, officials of the {{ill|lt=National Tax Agency|Q11421205|WD=Q11421205|1=National Tax Agency}} inspect it. On the day, sake brewers gather and hold a dedication rite praying for safety. The shrine receives yearly permission from Japan’s Ministry of Finance to brew up to one koku of {{ill|lt=doburoku|Q1371541|WD=Q1371541|1=Doburoku}} for the festival. === References === * Seki Kazuhiko, ''Commentary on Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki'', 2006, {{ill|lt=Akashi Shoten|Q11512528|WD=Q11512528|1=Akashi Shoten}} ISBN 4-7503-2376-4 * Takine Yoshiyuki, ''Dictionary of Ancient Izumo'', 2001, {{ill|lt=Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha|Q11501312|WD=Q11501312|1=Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha. "Rekishi Dokuhon" Henshūbu}} ISBN 4-404-02941-1 * Edited by {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Kenichi|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}}, ''Gods of Japan: Shrines and Sacred Places 7 San’in'' (new edition), 2000, {{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}} ISBN 978-4-560-02507-9 === Related literature === *{{Cite journal |和書|author =Hayami Yasutaka|title =Izumo Myth and Sake Brewing|date =1987|publisher =Brewing Society of Japan|journal =Journal of the Brewing Society of Japan|volume =82|issue =8|doi=10.6013/jbrewsocjapan1915.82.562|pages =562-566|ref = }} === Related items === *{{ill|lt=Matsuo Shrine|Q11529677|WD=Q11529677|1=Matsuo shrine}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Saka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Saka Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumo Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumo Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shimane Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Izumo]] [[Category:History of Izumo, Shimane]] [[Category:島根県の酒]]
